description_plain: Your child can change sentence structure to create effects and use references to help understand complex texts.

subject: Language Arts
description: Apply knowledge of language to understand how language functions in different contexts, to make effective choices for meaning or style, and to comprehend more fully when reading or listening.
description_plain: Your child understands how language works in different situations to choose words and styles that make meaning clear when reading, writing, or listening.

subject: Language Arts

description_plain: Your child can share information and evidence clearly, explain their point of view, address other opinions, and organize their ideas well for different audiences and purposes.

description_plain: Your child can listen carefully to different opinions, combine ideas and evidence from all sides, resolve disagreements when possible, and decide if more research is needed.

description: Make inferences about the intended audience and purpose of a variety of historical sources from information within the source itself (e.g., diary entries, historical markers, letters, seals, symbols).
description_plain: Your child can guess who a historical source was made for and why, using clues from the source itself.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare information provided by different historical sources about an event or issue in Connecticut’s history (e.g., patents, advertisements, paintings, illustrations, artifacts, newspaper articles, monuments, secondary sources).
description_plain: Your child can compare information from different historical sources about an event or issue in Connecticut’s history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Generate questions about individuals and groups who represented communities committed to change in Connecticut (e.g., Thomas Hooker, Nathan Hale, Rev. James W. C. Pennington, Sarah Harris and Prudence Crandall, Noah Webster, Sengbe Pieh, Laura Wheeler Waring, Yung Wing, Thirman Milner, María Colón Sánchez, Harriet Beecher Stowe).
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about people and groups in Connecticut who worked to make changes in their communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the lived experiences of diverse cultural groups by comparing life in specific historical time periods to life in Connecticut today (e.g., industry, local customs, migration, trade, traditions).

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the regional and cultural characteristics of Northeastern Woodland Native American tribes of Connecticut, present and past (e.g., customs, locations of tribal territories, material culture, oral histories).

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the impact of diverse cultures within a local community and throughout Connecticut (e.g., art, businesses, foodways, language, music, religion).

subject: Social Studies
description: Use both local and state maps of Connecticut to describe location of cultural and environmental characteristics over time (e.g., civic spaces, economic centers, place, population density, settlement patterns, waterways).
description_plain: Your child can use maps of Connecticut to show where important cultural and natural places are and how they have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Cultural Communities in Connecticut Present and Past
description_plain: Your child understands the different cultural communities that have lived in Connecticut in the past and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how services in communities are paid for by local, state, and federal taxes (e.g., income tax, local and state budgets, property tax, sales tax).
description_plain: Your child can explain how local, state, and federal taxes help pay for services in communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how Connecticut laws address public needs and concerns (e.g., education, safety, environmental issues, housing security, discrimination).

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop questions about historically significant regional and global economic events and issues (e.g., European Sovereign Debt Crisis, Association of Southeast Asian Nations, COVID-19 policy responses, Asia-Pacific Free Trade).
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about important economic events and issues in different regions and around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the benefits and costs of trade policies to individuals and groups in a region (e.g., Belt and Road Initiative, Schengen Area, African Continental Free Trade Area).
description_plain: Your child can explain the good and bad effects of trade rules on people and groups in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how economic decision-making in a region can affect the true cost of goods or services (e.g., carbon emissions, child and forced labor, strip mining, global waste trade).
description_plain: Your child understands how economic choices in a region can affect the real cost of goods, including environmental and social impacts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of competition in the determination of prices and wages in a region (e.g., consumer goods, fossil fuels, wages).
description_plain: Your child can describe how competition affects prices and wages in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of supply and demand within and among regions (e.g., oil, gold, chocolate, iron, coffee, gemstones, Tulip mania).
description_plain: Your child understands how supply and demand work within and between regions for things like oil, gold, and coffee.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how economic decisions affect the well-being of individuals, businesses, and societies within a region (e.g., Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, South Asia Food and Nutrition Security Initiative, National Human Development Initiative, FIFA World Cup).
description_plain: Your child can explain how economic decisions affect people, businesses, and societies in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how economic decisions influence the environment and daily life within and among regions (e.g., agriculture, energy production, population density, resource extraction, waste disposal).
description_plain: Your child understands how economic choices affect the environment and daily life in different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Economic Decision-Making and Exchanges
description_plain: Your child learns about how people make economic decisions and trade with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify a series of historical events and developments in a region as examples of political change and continuity (e.g., Fall of Rome, Korean Demilitarized Zone, Fall of Saigon, Partition of India).
description_plain: Your child can sort historical events in a region as examples of political changes or things that stayed the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare how individuals and groups have affected change in a region using historical and contemporary examples (e.g., World Wildlife Fund, Malala Yousafzai, Mohandas Gandhi, women’s rights in Saudi Arabia, Free Thai Movement).
description_plain: Your child can compare how people and groups have caused change in a region using examples from history and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how political organizations have shaped people’s lives in a region (e.g., International Monetary Fund, African Union, Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation, European Union, United Nations, World Health Organization).
description_plain: Your child can explain how groups like the United Nations or European Union affect people's lives in different parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how cultural characteristics have shaped political boundaries within a region (e.g., linguistics, religion, tribal affiliation).
description_plain: Your child understands how things like language, religion, or tribes have helped decide political borders in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how environmental characteristics have shaped political boundaries within a region (e.g., Pyrenees Mountains, Strait of Gibraltar, the Alps, Gobi Desert, Ural Mountains).
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural features like mountains or deserts have helped shape political borders in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Systems of Governance
description_plain: Systems of Governance

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the plausible maker, date, origin, and audience of a historical map using related historical sources (e.g., Eratosthenes’ World Map, Mappa Mundi, Mercator Projection, London’s cholera outbreak map, Ribeiro’s World Map).
description_plain: Your child can figure out who made a historical map, when and where it was made, and who it was for by looking at related history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how human characteristics of a region are connected to identities and cultures (e.g., built environment, language, world religions).
description_plain: Your child understands how things people build, the languages they speak, and their religions connect to their culture and identity in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how physical characteristics of a region are connected to identities and cultures (e.g., agriculture in Ukraine, Indian Diaspora, Ethiopian highlands).
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural features like farming areas or highlands relate to the culture and identity of people living there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the similarities and differences of cultural and environmental characteristics within and among regions (e.g., Abrahamic religions in Israel and Palestine, regional identity in India, Northern and Southern China).
description_plain: Your child can compare how culture and environment are similar or different within and between regions, like religions or regional identities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cultural and environmental characteristics that have shaped a region (e.g., climate in Northern vs. Southern Europe, pastoralist communities in South Asia).
description_plain: Your child can explain how culture and environment together shape what a region is like, such as climate or types of communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how cultural patterns influence the environment and daily life within and among regions (e.g., pollution of the Ganges River, Croatian access to Adriatic Seacoast, MainDanube waterway, Chinese New Year, Hajj).
description_plain: Your child understands how cultural habits affect the environment and daily life in different regions, like festivals or pollution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe changes in environmental characteristics of a region using maps and other representations in both historical and contemporary contexts (e.g., expansion of the Sahara Desert, urbanization in China, deforestation in Northern Europe).
description_plain: Your child can describe how a region's environment has changed over time using maps, like deserts growing or cities expanding.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship between the locations of places and regional characteristics using maps and other representations (e.g., religious centers, Nile River flooding, sea level rise in Venice, New Northwest Passage).
description_plain: Your child can explain how where places are located relates to their features, like religious sites or flooding areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship between population and bodies of water using maps and other representations of a region (e.g., Red Sea, Caspian Sea, Persian Gulf, Lake Victoria).
description_plain: Your child understands how people tend to live near bodies of water and can explain this using maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate spatial awareness by constructing maps to represent spatial patterns and environmental characteristics of a region (e.g., absolute location, built environment, natural features, population density, relative location).
description_plain: Your child can make maps that show where things are and what the environment is like in a region, including cities, natural features, and population.

subject: Social Studies
description: World Geographic Regions
description_plain: World Geographic Regions

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess individual and collective capacities to take action to address local, regional, and global problems, taking into account a range of strategies, and potential outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can think about how people alone or together can solve local or global problems by trying different ideas and thinking about results.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how a specific problem can manifest itself at local, regional, and global levels over time, identifying its characteristics and causes.
description_plain: Your child can study how a problem shows up in local, regional, and global places over time and explain what causes it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present arguments and explanations about a local, regional, or global problem to reach a target audience using print, oral, and digital technologies.
description_plain: Your child can share reasons and explanations about a problem using speaking, writing, or digital tools to reach others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Critique arguments and explanations presented about events or issues in a world region.
description_plain: Your child can think carefully about and judge the reasons and explanations others give about events or issues in a world region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct explanations using summary, sequence, and examples.
description_plain: Your child can explain ideas clearly by summarizing, putting events in order, and giving examples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments using evidence from multiple sources.
description_plain: Your child can build strong reasons by using facts from many different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop claims and counterclaims in response to a compelling question.
description_plain: Your child can make claims and also think about opposing ideas when answering important questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify relevant evidence from multiple sources to support claims.
description_plain: Your child can find useful facts from many sources to support their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Gather relevant evidence from multiple sources using both print and digital resources and databases (e.g., origin, authority, structure, context).
description_plain: Your child can gather important facts from books, websites, and databases, checking who made them and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply disciplinary knowledge and practices to demonstrate an understanding of world regional studies context.
description_plain: Your child uses what they know about social studies to understand different world regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Determine the kinds of sources that will be helpful in answering compelling and supporting questions, taking into consideration multiple points of views represented in the sources.
description_plain: Your child can decide which sources will help answer big and smaller questions, thinking about different viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how compelling and supporting questions are mutually reinforcing.
description_plain: Your child understands how big questions and smaller supporting questions work together to learn about a topic.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how compelling and supporting questions represent key ideas about geography or other social science disciplines in the study of a world region.
description_plain: Your child understands how important questions help explore key ideas about geography and social studies in a world region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how a trade barrier has shaped global or regional trade in historical and contemporary contexts (e.g., tariff barrier, non-tariff barrier, embargo).
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules like tariffs or embargoes have affected trade between countries in the past and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how improvements in productivity enhance the standard of living in a region (e.g., automation, computer processing, 4-day workweek).
description_plain: Your child understands how making work more efficient, like using machines or shorter workweeks, can improve people's lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe tools and resources that are used to measure standard of living (e.g., Gross Domestic Product, Human Development Index).
description_plain: Your child can describe tools that measure how well people live, like GDP or the Human Development Index.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how economic fluctuations and cycles affect individuals and groups in a region (e.g., deflation, inflation, unemployment, depression, recession).
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in the economy, like inflation or unemployment, affect people in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of labor unions, corporations, and non-profits within the market economy of a region (e.g., representation, collective bargaining, production, investment, social impact, advocacy).
description_plain: Your child understands the roles of labor unions, companies, and non-profits in how a region's economy works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of innovation and entrepreneurship in a market economy (e.g., mills, transportation, electricity, computer technology, research and development, knowledge economy).
description_plain: Your child can explain how new ideas and starting businesses help grow a market economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how changes in supply and demand have influenced the price and quantity of a good or service in a region (e.g., gold inflation related to Mansa Musa’s Hajj, Dutch fur trade in North America, Rainforest logging, Cobalt mining in Democratic Republic of the Congo, global agriculture, Venezuela oil production, illegal poaching).
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in supply and demand affect prices and amounts of goods or services in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways in which money facilitates the exchange of goods and services in both historical and contemporary contexts (e.g., cattle, Cowrie shells, wampum, coins, banknotes, credit cards, cryptocurrency).
description_plain: Your child understands how money, in many forms like coins or credit cards, helps people trade goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of competition in a market economy (e.g., prices, wages, tariffs, trade agreements, international trade organizations).
description_plain: Your child can describe how competition affects prices, wages, and trade in a market economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe different types of economies (e.g., command, market, mixed).
description_plain: Your child can describe different types of economies, like those controlled by the government or by the market.

subject: Social Studies
description: Regional and Global Economics
description_plain: Regional and Global Economics

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how norms regarding gender and sexuality influence the daily lives of people (e.g., LGBTQ+ rights, family structures, matrilineal societies, division of labor, political participation).
description_plain: Your child understands how ideas about gender and sexuality affect people's daily lives, families, and roles in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe a series of political events and developments that reflect change and continuity throughout the history of a region (e.g., trans-Saharan Migration, Spanish Conquest of Hispaniola, Mexican Revolution, Cuban Revolution, Latin American independence, Apartheid, Central African Republic Civil War, Yogyakarta Principles, Timbuktu).
description_plain: Your child can describe important political events that show how a region has changed or stayed the same over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose, implementation, and consequences of a policy in both historical and contemporary contexts (e.g., environmental, human rights, public health, White Australia Policy).
description_plain: Your child can explain why a policy was made, how it was carried out, and what effects it had in the past and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the roles of political and economic organizations in shaping lives of individuals and groups in a region (e.g., World Bank, United Nations, International Monetary Fund, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, World Trade Organization).
description_plain: Your child can describe how political and economic groups like the World Bank or United Nations affect people's lives in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify the kinds of historical sources used in a secondary interpretations of a globally or regionally significant event or issue (e.g., genocide, natural disasters, climate change, refugee crises, human trafficking).
description_plain: Your child can sort different types of historical sources used to explain important global or regional events like genocide or climate change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the effectiveness of rules, laws, and agreements as a means of addressing regional and global problems (e.g., International Court of Justice, International Criminal Court, Paris Agreement, International Atomic Energy Agency).
description_plain: Your child will judge how well rules, laws, and agreements work to solve big regional and global problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political participation varies within different types of government in different eras and contexts (e.g., compulsory voting, protest, volunteerism, women’s suffrage).
description_plain: Your child understands how people take part in politics differently depending on the type of government and time period.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the powers and responsibilities of individuals, groups, and media in addressing human rights issues in both governmental and nongovernmental contexts (e.g., Universal Declaration of Human Rights, International Refugee Assistance, United States Border Security, United Nations).
description_plain: Your child can name the roles and duties of people, groups, and the media in handling human rights issues in governments and other organizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Governance and Human Rights
description_plain: Governance and Human Rights

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how maps represent the perspectives of individuals and groups during different eras (e.g., Treaty of Tordesillas map, Catalan Atlas, Mercator Projection, Carte du Congo Belge).
description_plain: Your child can explain how maps show the views of different people and groups from various times in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how global changes in population distribution patterns have affected land use in a region (e.g., the Pampas, Deforestation in the Amazon, Southwest United States population boom).
description_plain: Your child understands how changes in where people live around the world affect how land is used in certain areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how environmental characteristics vary among regions (e.g., climate, natural disasters, waterways, mountain ranges, deserts).
description_plain: Your child can compare how natural features like climate and mountains differ between regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how cultural characteristics vary among regions and shape diversity of local communities (e.g., cultural centers, diasporas, cultural universals, taboos, linguistics).
description_plain: Your child understands how culture varies in different regions and how this creates diverse local communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the influences of long-term human-induced environmental change on spatial patterns of conflict and cooperation within a region (e.g., State Barrier Fence of Western Australia, uncontacted Indigenous tribes, Third Agricultural Revolution).
description_plain: Your child can explain how long-term human changes to the environment affect conflicts and cooperation in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human-environment interactions influence movement and settlement (e.g., Floating City Of Ganvie, The Dry Corridor, Tenochtitlán, Silk Road, trans-Saharan caravans).
description_plain: Your child understands how people’s interactions with the environment influence where they move and settle.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how growth in communication technology has led to the diffusion of ideas and cultural practices (e.g., Hollywood, global news networks, television, telecommunication infrastructure, social media).
description_plain: Your child can describe how advances in communication technology help spread ideas and cultures around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how changes in transportation have led to regional and global connections among human settlements (e.g., United States Interstate Highway System, Panama Canal, Mombasa– Nairobi Standard Gauge Railway).
description_plain: Your child understands how improvements in transportation connect people and places regionally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze cultural diffusion among regions using paper based and digital mapping techniques (e.g., expansion, relocation).
description_plain: Your child can use maps to study how cultures spread and change across regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Movement and Cultural Diffusion
description_plain: Your child understands how people and cultures move and spread around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess individual and collective capacities to take action to address local, regional, and global problems, taking into account a range of possible levers of power, strategies, and potential outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can think about how individuals and groups can take action to solve problems at local, regional, and global levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how a specific problem can manifest itself at local, regional, and global levels over time, identifying its characteristics and causes, and the challenges and opportunities faced by those trying to address the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how a problem can appear in different places over time and what makes it hard or possible to fix.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present arguments and explanations about local, regional, and global issues to reach a target audience using print, oral, and digital technologies.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas and reasons about issues using speaking, writing, and digital tools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Critique arguments and explanations presented about events or issues in a world region.
description_plain: Your child can think critically about arguments and explanations about events or issues in a world region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct explanations using summary, sequence, examples, and data.
description_plain: Your child can explain ideas clearly using summaries, examples, and data.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments using evidence from multiple sources.
description_plain: Your child can build arguments using evidence from many sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop claims and counterclaims in response to a compelling question.
description_plain: Your child can create claims and opposing claims when answering important questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Organize and prioritize relevant evidence from multiple sources to support claims.
description_plain: Your child can organize and choose the best evidence from different sources to support their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Gather relevant evidence from multiple sources using both print and digital resources and databases (e.g., origin, authority, structure, context, credibility).
description_plain: Your child can find useful evidence from books, websites, and databases, checking if the sources are trustworthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply disciplinary knowledge and practices to demonstrate an understanding of world regional studies content.
description_plain: Your child uses knowledge and skills from social studies to understand world regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Determine the kinds of sources that will be helpful in answering compelling and supporting questions, taking into consideration multiple points of views represented in the sources.
description_plain: Your child can decide which sources will help answer important questions, considering different viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop compelling and supporting questions that are mutually reinforcing.
description_plain: Your child can create main and supporting questions that work well together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how compelling and supporting questions represent key ideas about geography or other social science disciplines in the study of a world region.
description_plain: Your child understands how important questions help explore key ideas about geography and social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the successes and failures of the Reconstruction in fulfilling ideas and principles contained in the founding documents (e.g., elected Black officials, Ebenezer Bassett, Freedmen’s Bureau, Historically Black Colleges and Universities, Compromise of 1877).
description_plain: Your child can judge how well the Reconstruction period met the goals in the country’s founding documents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purposes, implementation, and consequences of policies and political strategies during the Reconstruction Era (e.g., Homestead Act, Second Treaty of Fort Laramie, women’s suffrage, Radical Reconstruction).
description_plain: Your child understands the reasons for and effects of policies and political actions during Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of racism both during and after Reconstruction (e.g., Page Act, Ku Klux Klan, Black Codes).
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes and effects of racism during and after Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of the Reconstruction Amendments (e.g., abolition, Latino and Black citizenship, suffrage).
description_plain: Your child understands why the Reconstruction Amendments were made and what effects they had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why individuals challenged gender roles in the Civil War Era (e.g., Loreta Janeta Velazquez, Frances Louisa Clayton, Anna Elizabeth Dickinson, Hartford Soldiers’ Aid Society, Rebecca Primus and Addie Brown).
description_plain: Your child can explain how some people challenged traditional gender roles during the Civil War era.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the factors that shaped perspectives in Connecticut about the Civil War (e.g., free labor movement, role of government, abolition).
description_plain: Your child understands what shaped people’s views about the Civil War in Connecticut.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze factors that influenced individual and group perspectives about national identity both during and as a result of the Civil War (e.g., Copperheads, Republicans, Wide Awakes, Unionists, women in the industrial workforce).
description_plain: Your child can explain how different groups and individuals saw national identity during and after the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how policies related to military service shaped the events and outcomes of the Civil War (e.g., conscription, Emancipation Proclamation, draft riots, Connecticut 29th, Massachusetts 54th).
description_plain: Your child understands how military service policies affected the events and results of the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how change and continuity in technology, infrastructure, and military strategies shaped the Civil War (e.g., rail, scorched earth, blockade, Colt Firearms, Sharps Rifles).
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in technology and military strategies influenced the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare historical means used by individuals and groups to promote, sustain, and dismantle discrimination in the Antebellum Period (e.g., Underground Railroad, Bleeding Kansas, American Anti-Slavery Society, American Colonization Society, Compromise of 1850).
description_plain: Your child can compare how people and groups worked to support or fight discrimination before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the relative influence of political, economic, and social developments that contributed to secession (e.g., Free Soil Movement, Nullification, Dred Scott Decision, Harper’s Ferry, abolition).
description_plain: Your child can explain how political, economic, and social events contributed to states leaving the Union.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using multiple historical sources, identify individuals and groups during the Antebellum Period who sustained, promoted, or challenged the institution of slavery (e.g., Frederick Douglass, Harriett Beecher Stowe, John C. Calhoun).
description_plain: Your child can identify people and groups who supported or opposed slavery before the Civil War using different historical sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how secession sought to preserve the social, economic, and racial hierarchies throughout the United States (e.g., domestic labor, manufacturing, slavocracy, textiles).
description_plain: Your child understands how secession aimed to keep social, economic, and racial inequalities in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civil Unrest, Civil War, and Reconstruction
description_plain: Your child understands the causes and effects of civil unrest, the Civil War, and the Reconstruction period in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of innovation and entrepreneurship at the start of the American Industrial Revolution (e.g., mills, patents, telegraph, interchangeable parts).
description_plain: Your child can explain how new ideas and businesses, like mills and inventions, helped start the American Industrial Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the demand for finished goods influenced business specialization and the growing need for labor during the American Industrial Revolution (e.g., textiles, firearms, shipbuilding, manufacturing, foundries, logging).
description_plain: Your child understands how the need for finished products led businesses to specialize and hire more workers during the Industrial Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the transition from an agricultural-based economy to an industrialized economy affected the well-being of individuals, groups, and businesses (e.g., factory workers, child laborers, women in the industrial workforce, formerly enslaved persons, Irish, Chinese, Black, and White laborers and tradespeople).
description_plain: Your child can explain how moving from farming to factory work changed the lives of workers, including women, children, and different racial groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how Westward Expansion increased access to natural resources and the production and distribution of goods in a global market (e.g., agriculture, mining, canals, factories, rail, shipping).
description_plain: Your child understands how moving west helped people get natural resources and sell goods around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the influence human-induced environmental change had on spatial patterns of industrialization and Westward Expansion (e.g., Cumberland and National Roads, Erie Canal, Farmington Canal, Pacific Railroad).
description_plain: Your child can explain how human changes to the environment, like building roads and canals, affected where industries and settlements grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare movements for social change and the strategies they employed by the United States in the Antebellum Period (e.g., abolition, Second Great Awakening, suffrage, temperance).
description_plain: Your child compares different social change movements before the Civil War and how they worked to make changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze attempts by individuals and groups with nativist views to shape the United States in the Antebellum Period (e.g., Philadelphia Riots of 1844, Know Nothing Party).
description_plain: Your child understands how groups with nativist views tried to influence the U.S. before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of government policies and executive decisions related to industrialization and expansion of the United States (e.g., Bureau of Indian Affairs, Indian Removal Act, Louisiana Purchase, Mexican-American War).
description_plain: Your child explains how government actions and decisions affected industrial growth and U.S. expansion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop questions using multiple historical sources about how individuals and groups in northern states sustained or challenged racial inequality during the Antebellum Period (e.g., Connecticut’s gradual emancipation act, Amistad Trials, Sarah Harris and Prudence Crandall, The Liberator, American Colonization Society, American Anti-Slavery Society, Underground Railroad).
description_plain: Your child asks questions using different historical sources about how people in northern states supported or fought against racial inequality before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the perspectives of immigrant groups and their reasons for moving to and within the United States (e.g., German, Irish, Scandinavian, Chinese, Jewish).
description_plain: Your child understands why different immigrant groups came to and moved within the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop questions to analyze the significance of the methods of resistance employed by enslaved and free Black people in the United States (e.g., work resistance, violence, sabotage, petition, forming maroon communities, independent churches, and educational associations).
description_plain: Your child develops questions to study how enslaved and free Black people resisted unfair treatment in different ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the events and developments related to the institution of slavery in the early republic (e.g., Haitian Revolution, Act Prohibiting the Importation of Slaves, cotton gin, textile industry).
description_plain: Your child explains important events and changes related to slavery in the early United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of United States’ treaties on the sovereignty of Native nations (e.g., Ordinance of 1787, Treaty of the Creeks, Treaty of Greenville, Louisiana Purchase, Treaty of Fort Wayne).
description_plain: Your child explains how U.S. treaties affected the independence and land of Native nations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Expansion, Influence, and Economic Growth
description_plain: Your child understands how the United States grew in size, influence, and economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the US Constitution and Bill of Rights to determine if the goals of the American Revolution, as stated in the Declaration of Independence, were realized.
description_plain: Your child evaluates if the U.S. Constitution and Bill of Rights achieved the goals of the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relevance of democratic principles in shaping acts of dissent and resistance in early United States history (e.g., Whiskey Rebellion, Shay’s Rebellion, Nat Turner’s Rebellion).
description_plain: Your child explains how democratic ideas influenced early acts of protest and rebellion in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how ideas and principles about citizenship and access to political power expressed in founding documents influenced social and political systems in the early republic (e.g., Bill of Rights, Declaration of Independence, Three-Fifths Clause, Electoral College).
description_plain: Your child analyzes how ideas about citizenship and political power in founding documents shaped early U.S. society and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the ideas and principles within the Bill of Rights influence the social and political system in the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Bill of Rights affects the social and political systems in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the perspectives, civic virtues, and democratic principles debated during the Constitutional Convention (e.g., Connecticut Compromise, federalism, Three-Fifths Compromise).
description_plain: Your child explains the different views and democratic ideas debated during the writing of the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the evolving powers and limits of the three branches of government as defined in the United States Constitution and subsequent federal court rulings, legislation, and executive action (e.g., Alien and Sedition Acts, Marbury v. Madison, Treaty of San Lorenzo).
description_plain: Your child explains how the powers and limits of the three government branches have changed over time through laws and court decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the origin, purpose, and impact of the founding documents (e.g., popular sovereignty, republicanism, separation of powers, enfranchisement, liberty).
description_plain: Your child studies the purpose and effects of the founding documents like the Declaration of Independence and Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain laws that institutionalized slavery in the Colonial Era and in the Constitution (e.g., Black Laws of Virginia, Fugitive Slave Act, Three-Fifths Clause).
description_plain: Your child explains laws that supported slavery during colonial times and in the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how individual and group perspectives of the American Revolution are documented in historical records while identifying representation of marginalized voices (e.g., journals, letters, pamphlets, newspaper articles from sources such as the Hartford Daily Courant).
description_plain: Your child describes how different people’s views of the American Revolution are shown in historical records, including those often left out.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze factors that influenced perspectives about national identity both during and as a result of the American Revolution (e.g., social contract, individual liberties, tyranny, economic and political sovereignty, citizenship).
description_plain: Your child analyzes what shaped ideas about national identity during and after the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the perspectives of both enslaved and free Black people through the historical sources they created (e.g., Venture Smith, Phyllis Wheatley, Olaudah Equiano).
description_plain: Your child studies the views of enslaved and free Black people by reading the historical writings they created.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the events and developments that led to the establishment of colonies in the Atlantic and Pacific Worlds (e.g., desire for religious liberty, natural resources, economic opportunities, technological advancements).
description_plain: Your child explains the events that led to the founding of colonies in the Atlantic and Pacific regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Origins of United States Democracy and National Identity
description_plain: Your child understands how U.S. democracy and national identity began.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply a range of deliberative and democratic procedures to make decisions and take action in classrooms, schools, and out-of-school civic contexts.
description_plain: Your child practices using fair and democratic ways to make decisions and take action in school and community settings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how a specific problem can manifest itself at local, regional, and global levels over time, identifying its characteristics and causes, and the challenges and opportunities faced by those trying to address the problem.
description_plain: Your child analyzes how a problem can appear locally, regionally, and globally over time, including its causes and challenges.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present arguments and explanations that feature multiple perspectives about local, state, or national history to reach a target audience using print, oral, and digital technologies.
description_plain: Your child presents arguments about history using different viewpoints and shares them through writing, speaking, or digital tools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Critique the credibility of arguments and structure of explanations related to a historical account of an event or issue in United States history.
description_plain: Your child evaluates how trustworthy and well-organized arguments are about historical events or issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct explanations using reasoning, summary, sequence, examples, and data while pointing out the strengths and weaknesses of the explanations.
description_plain: Your child explains ideas clearly using reasons, examples, and data, and points out what works well and what doesn’t.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments using evidence from multiple sources, while acknowledging the strengths and limitations of the arguments.
description_plain: Your child builds arguments using evidence from many sources and recognizes their strengths and weaknesses.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop claims and counterclaims while pointing out the strengths and limitations of different sources.
description_plain: Your child creates claims and opposing claims while noting the strengths and limits of different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Organize and prioritize relevant evidence from and among multiple sources to support claims.
description_plain: Your child organizes and chooses important evidence from many sources to support their claims.

subject: Social Studies
description: Gather information from multiple sources and evaluate their relevance and intended use (e.g., origin, authority, structure, context, corroborative value, credibility).
description_plain: Your child gathers information from many sources and decides how useful and trustworthy they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply disciplinary knowledge and practices to demonstrate an understanding of United States history content.
description_plain: Your child uses knowledge and skills from social studies to understand U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Determine the kinds of sources that will be helpful in answering compelling and supporting questions, taking into consideration multiple points of views represented in the sources.
description_plain: Your child figures out which sources will help answer important questions, considering different viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop compelling and supporting questions that are mutually reinforcing.
description_plain: Your child creates important questions and supporting questions that work well together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how compelling and supporting questions represent key ideas in the study of United States history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important questions help us understand key ideas in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
